epigraph.text: The Lord is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth.
epigraph.quote: Every analytic function is locally a power series—a fact unique to complex analysis.

content.text:
We now prove that analytic functions can be represented by Taylor series 
—that analyticity and power series representation are equivalent.

content.title: Taylor's Theorem
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain containing the closed disk 
$\overline{D}(z_0, R)$. Then for all $z$ with $|z - z_0| < R$:
\[
 f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n(z - z_0)^n, \quad 
 \text{where } a_n = \frac{f^{(n)}(z_0)}{n!}.
\]
The series converges absolutely and uniformly on compact subsets.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By the Cauchy Integral Formula, for $|z - z_0| < r < R$:
\[
 f(z) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{|w-z_0|=r} \frac{f(w)}{w - z}\,dw.
\]
Write
\[
 \frac{1}{w - z} = \frac{1}{(w - z_0) - (z - z_0)} 
 = \frac{1}{w - z_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{z - z_0}{w - z_0}}.
\]
Since $|z - z_0| < r = |w - z_0|$, we can expand:
\[
 \frac{1}{1 - \frac{z - z_0}{w - z_0}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{z - z_0}{w - z_0}\right)^n.
\]
Substituting and exchanging sum and integral:
\[
 f(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (z - z_0)^n \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{|w-z_0|=r} \frac{f(w)}{(w - z_0)^{n+1}}\,dw.
\]
By the Cauchy derivative formula, the integral equals $f^{(n)}(z_0)/n!$.

content.title: Standard Taylor Series
content.content:
The following hold for all $z \in \C$:
$$
 e^z &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^n}{n!} = 1 + z + \frac{z^2}{2!} + \frac{z^3}{3!} + \cdots \\
 \sin z &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n z^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!} = z - \frac{z^3}{3!} + \frac{z^5}{5!} - \cdots \\
 \cos z &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n z^{2n}}{(2n)!} = 1 - \frac{z^2}{2!} + \frac{z^4}{4!} - \cdots \\
 \sinh z &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!}, \quad
 \cosh z = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^{2n}}{(2n)!}
$$
For $|z| < 1$:
\[
 \frac{1}{1-z} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} z^n, \quad
 \Log(1+z) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n+1}z^n}{n}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Identity Theorem
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are analytic on a domain $D$ and $f(z_n) = g(z_n)$ for a 
sequence $z_n \to z^* \in D$, then $f = g$ on all of $D$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This remarkable result says that an analytic function is determined by 
its values on any sequence with a limit point in the domain.

content.content:
Two analytic functions agreeing on an interval automatically agree on 
the largest connected domain containing that interval where both are 
defined. This is the principle of **analytic continuation**.

marginNotes.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God, and 
the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

marginNotes.author: Brook Taylor (1715)
marginNotes.text:
The series for any function in powers 
of its argument.
